text: The transabdominal view reveals bilateral direct inguinal hernia defects, visible after the placement of robotic trocars through the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon performs a limited incision in the right lower quadrant to address bowel adhesions, preparing to extend the peritoneal incision during the dissection.
The surgeon attempts to use a monopolar scissor, which is visibly ineffective at coagulating micro vessels in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the midline, performing limited lateral dissection as part of the procedure.
The surgeon works within the visceral plane, reducing the directorneas by pushing away the preperitoneal fat and pseudosac, which descend smoothly without complications.

The surgeon identifies Hasselbach's ligament on the lateral border of the rectus muscle, highlighting the anatomical structures. The dissection progresses in the retropubic space, ensuring at least two centimeters of separation between the pubis and the bladder.

The surgeon identifies the conar ligament and observes natural fat over the obliterated umbilical artery, which is part of the preperitoneal portion of the medial umbilical ligament. The fat is prominent and does not hinder the ability to lay a flat mesh. The surgeon works on parietalization of the vessels and develops an adequate pocket for mesh placement, highlighting the tubular structure of the obliterated umbilical artery.
The surgeon identifies the iliopsoas muscle and proceeds to take tissue well away from the sidewall to minimize bleeding risk, ensuring any potential bleeding can be easily controlled.
The surgeon finalizes the dissection on the left side, ensuring a clear separation of tissue layers and structures.

The surgeon visualizes the iliopubic tract and performs additional dissection around the obturator area, ensuring space for mesh placement. The absence of a lipoma is noted before proceeding to the contralateral side.

The surgeon observes adhesions in the preperitoneal plane, likely due to a past episode of appendicitis or diverticulitis, and confirms laparoscopically that the bowel is not adhered, allowing for safe dissection.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision and performs a similar dissection on the contralateral side. The bowel is visible over the hernia space, but there is no indication of an indirect hernia in this patient.
The surgeon dissects the fat away, revealing the obturator vessels in the surgical field.

The surgeon reorients the surgical field, revealing a thorough dissection with visible parietalization. Fat is seen overlying the obliterated umbilical artery, with careful dissection avoiding thermal injury due to the proximity of the bowel. The iliopsoas muscle and obturator are clearly visible, demonstrating successful bilateral dissection.
The surgeon identifies the iliopubic tract, ensuring no suspicious fat is present, and prepares the surgical field for mesh placement and fixation.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to coagulate microvessels near Cooper's ligament to prevent bleeding during stitch placement. Two extra-large meshes are positioned to overlap at the midline, resembling a Venn diagram.

The surgeon fortifies the central abdominal wall and performs flap closure using two six-inch V-lock sutures. The sutures are placed laterally to medially on both sides, extending just beyond the midline on one side and then sewing to it from the other side.
The surgeon completes the flap closure with no visible tension, achieving a straightforward result. The bilateral angles are clearly visible and appear to work effectively in this scenario.
